article.title: StWRKY65 stimulates thermotolerance in potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) through antioxidant and photosynthetic modulation

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects. PMC12319013.p002.s011: This study demonstrates the pivotal role of StWRKY65 in enhancing potato thermotolerance by simultaneously boosting antioxidant defenses and maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under heat stress. PMC12319013.p002.s012: As climate change intensifies thermal extremes, these findings position StWRKY65 as a valuable genetic target for developing heat-resistant potato varieties, offering a timely strategy to protect this vital crop and global food security.

events.event_label: Elevated temperatures increase development rates and respiration
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p004.s006: The impacts of climate change on potato production are complex, with studies yielding varied results. PMC12319013.p004.s007: Elevated temperatures may reduce yields by accelerating development rates and increasing respiration. PMC12319013.p004.s008: High temperatures (>30°C) severely impair potato growth and yield, causing physical tuber damage, reduced starch content, and premature sprouting. PMC12319013.p004.s009: As temperate crops, potatoes thrive at 16–19°C but tolerate 25–30°C. PMC12319013.p004.s010: Temperatures exceeding 30°C halt developmental processes by inducing stomatal closure.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances thermotolerance and antioxidant/photosynthetic modulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p001.s001: StWRKY65 stimulates thermotolerance in potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) through antioxidant and photosynthetic modulation

events.event_label: GFP localizes to plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances antioxidant defense by increasing APX, POD, SOD activities and reducing ROS
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p046.s001: To investigate the physiological responses of transgenic and NT potato lines of the cultivars ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ under different temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), the key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), oxidative stress marker (H2O2 and MDA), osmoprotectant proline, and total chlorophyll content were compared between NT and transgenic lines (OE and RNAi), as shown in Figures 6 , 7 . PMC12319013.p046.s002: Under the control condition (20°C), no significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed in all the aforementioned physiological indicators between transgenic and NT plants. PMC12319013.p046.s003: Under the conditions of 30°C and 35°C, the activities of APX ( Figures 6 , 7A ), CAT ( Figures 6 , 7B ), POD ( Figures 6 , 7C ), and SOD ( Figures 6 , 7D ) in the OE lines were significantly higher than those in the NT and RNAi lines (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), indicating that the overexpression of StWRKY65 could enhance the antioxidant defense system and more efficiently scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) under heat stress. PMC12319013.p046.s004: Meanwhile, the contents of oxidative stress markers H2O2 ( Figures 6 , 7E ) and MDA ( Figures 6 , 7F ) were also analyzed.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances antioxidant defenses and osmolyte accumulation under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). PMC12319013.p059.s007: In contrast, StWRKY65 RNAi lines showed the opposite trend.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 regulates growth traits and heat tolerance under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s006: Conversely, RNAi lines were highly sensitive to heat stress, showing drastic reductions in plant height (51.38%–61.70%), plant fresh and dry weights (57.77%–65.16% and 50.61%–59.24%), root fresh and dry weights (46.53%–56.27% and 43.28%–57.82%), and tuber weight (92.32%–94.91%) ( Figures 4A–F ), compared to NT lines. PMC12319013.p042.s007: Similar trends were observed in ‘Desiree’ plants under heat stress: at 30°C, StWRKY65-overexpression lines showed increases in plant height (26.70%–32.47%), plant fresh weight (26.35%–33.14%), plant dry weight (24.61%–30.12%), root fresh weight (25.58%–35.22%), root dry weight (29.50%–37.48%), and tuber weight (45.49%–56.31%) compared with NT controls, while RNAi lines exhibited decreases in these parameters (26.32%–32.03% for plant height, 27.01%–34.85% for plant fresh weight, 25.11%–32.74% for plant dry weight, 25.54%–32.52% for root fresh weight, 29.77%–36.34% for root dry weight, and 46.58%–57.86% for tuber weight) ( Figures 5A–F ). PMC12319013.p042.s008: At 35°C, OE lines further demonstrated enhanced heat tolerance (plant height +39.12%–49.66%, plant fresh/dry weights +40.37%–48.89%/+38.06%–53.09%, root fresh/dry weights +40.32%–50.97%/+48.52%–65.38%, tuber weight +79.54%–98.74%), whereas RNAi lines suffered severe growth inhibition (plant height −38.69%–46.15%, plant fresh/dry weights −38.86%–47.60%/−39.55%–41.60%, root fresh/dry weights −41.29%–50.94%/−49.17%–61.71%, tuber weight −78.90%–95.60%), compared to NT potato lines ( Figures 5A–F ). PMC12319013.p042.s009: Collectively, these results indicate that...

events.event_label: StWRKY65 up‑regulates antioxidant genes under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

events.event_label: WRKY TFs confer tolerance to heat, drought and salinity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p004.s012: Plants employ adaptive mechanisms, including stress-responsive TFs like MYB, AP2/ERFBP, NAC, and WRKY, to mitigate high-temperature damage. PMC12319013.p004.s013: Among these, WRKY TFs play a pivotal role in regulating pathways that combat diverse abiotic stresses, such as heat, drought, and salinity.

events.event_label: WRKY, MYB, AP2/ERFBP and NAC confer tolerance to heat stress

PMC12319013.p004.s011: Multiple studies confirm that heat stress disproportionately affects potato productivity compared to other climatic factors. PMC12319013.p004.s012: Plants employ adaptive mechanisms, including stress-responsive TFs like MYB, AP2/ERFBP, NAC, and WRKY, to mitigate high-temperature damage. PMC12319013.p004.s013: Among these, WRKY TFs play a pivotal role in regulating pathways that combat diverse abiotic stresses, such as heat, drought, and salinity.

events.event_label: Heat stress increases multiple growth traits in StWRKY65 overexpression lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s001: Growth index analysis demonstrated significant differences in heat tolerance between transgenic and NT potato lines under distinct heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p042.s002: Under control conditions (20°C), no significant differences (P>0.05) were detected in growth indices, including plant height, plant fresh and dry weights, root fresh and dry weights, and tuber weight, among genotypes of both the ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ varieties, as illustrated in Figures 4 , 5 . PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ).

PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ). PMC12319013.p042.s005: Under severe 35°C heat stress, the thermoprotective effect of StWRKY65 in ‘Atlantic’ became more pronounced: OE lines displayed remarkable stress resistance, with plant height increased by 51.10%–64.90%, plant fresh and dry weights by 49.38%–57.67% and 36.11%–36.93%, respectively, root fresh and dry weights by 46.42%–63.97% and 43.15%–57.38%, respectively, and tuber weight by 77.56%–97.53% relative to NT controls. PMC12319013.p042.s006: Conversely, RNAi lines were highly sensitive to heat stress, showing drastic reductions in plant height (51.38%–61.70%), plant fresh and dry weights (57.77%–65.16% and 50.61%–59.24%), root fresh and dry weights (46.53%–56.27% and 43.28%–57.82%), and tuber weight (92.32%–94.91%) ( Figures 4A–F ), compared to NT lines. events.event_label: StWRKY65 affects multiple growth parameters in 'Desiree' cultivar
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p044.s001: StWRKY65 modulates potato growth parameters of cultivar ‘Desiree’; (A) plant height, (B) fresh weight, (C) dry weight, (D) root fresh weight, (E) root dry weight, and (F) tuber yield per plant, after exposure to 20°C, 30°C, and 35°C of heat stress treatments. PMC12319013.p044.s002: NT, non-transgenic plants; OE, pBI121-EGFP-StWRKY65-transgenic plants (OE-1, OE-3, and OE-4); RNAi, pART-StWRKY65-RNAi-transgenic plants (RNAi-2, RNAi-3 and RNAi-5). PMC12319013.p044.s003: The data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. PMC12319013.p044.s004: P-values (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01) were calculated through ordinary two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test with a sample size of n = 9.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases antioxidant enzyme activities and metabolite levels while decreasing oxidative markers
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

events.event_label: CaWRKY40 enhances thermotolerance/heat resistance when overexpressed and reduces thermotolerance when lost
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p005.s015: In rice (Oryza sativa), OsWRKY11 enhanced drought tolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s016: In tomato (Solanum lycopersicum), SlWRKY80 plays a crucial role in enabling transgenic tomato plants to tolerate saline-alkali stress more effectively than wild-type plants. PMC12319013.p005.s017: In pepper (Capsicum annuum), CaWRKY40 enhances thermotolerance, with overexpression in tobacco improving heat resistance while its loss reduces tolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s018: However, the specific functions and regulatory mechanisms of potato WRKY family members (especially StWRKY65) in heat stress response have not yet been elucidated.

events.event_label: BrWRKY65 expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells. PMC12319013.p057.s014: Confocal microscopy showed nuclear-specific GFP fluorescence, co-localizing with DAPI-stained nuclei, while free GFP displayed diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear distribution. PMC12319013.p057.s015: These results confirm BrWRKY65’s nuclear localization, consistent with our investigation.

events.event_label: Heat stress impairs photosynthetic apparatus and efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s001: Extreme heat stress disrupts the photosynthetic apparatus in plant cells, leading to a significant decline in photosynthetic efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s002: Our findings reveal that sustained exposure to elevated temperatures (30–35°C) induced significant photosynthetic impairment in both RNAi and NT potato plants. PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage.

events.event_label: NaCl associated with salt stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p035.s001: Under salt stress, ‘Desiree’ exhibited fluctuating downregulation with 75 mM NaCl treatment (significant declines at 1 and 3 h, recovery at 6 and 12 h, a sharp drop at 24 h, and partial recovery at 48 h, reaching the lowest level at 24 h). PMC12319013.p035.s002: High salinity (150 mM NaCl) caused more pronounced downregulation (continuous declines at 1, 3, and 6 h, a slight rebound at 12 h, followed by further decreases at 24 and 48 h, with the lowest level at 6 h) ( Figures 2I–K ). PMC12319013.p035.s003: In ‘Atlantic’, 75 mM NaCl triggered a biphasic response: initial downregulation (lowest at 1 h), followed by upregulation (peak at 3 h), then declines at 6 h and rebounds at 12 h, before significant downregulation at 24 and 48 h ( Figure 2J ). PMC12319013.p035.s004: Meanwhile, 150 mM NaCl predominantly induced downregulation (notably at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 24 h, with recovery at 48 h, lowest at 24 h) ( Figure 2L ), though the suppression was less severe than with 75 mM NaCl ( Figures 2I–L ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases plant height, tuber yield, and biomass under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: WRKY TFs are detected in Arabidopsis, tobacco and rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s001: WRKY TFs typically exhibit tissue-specific expression patterns and are influenced by environmental stresses, enabling them to regulate diverse biological processes in plants. PMC12319013.p057.s002: For example, in pepper (Capsicum annuum), CaWRKY40 is upregulated under heat stress and acts as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p057.s003: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in thermotolerance, we analyzed its expression profile in the leaf tissues of two potato cultivars, Atlantic and Desiree ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p057.s004: qRT-PCR-based expression profiling revealed cultivar-specific differential regulation of StWRKY65 under heat stress (30°C and 35°C) at multiple time points.

events.event_label: BrWRKY65 localizes to nuclei in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells. PMC12319013.p057.s014: Confocal microscopy showed nuclear-specific GFP fluorescence, co-localizing with DAPI-stained nuclei, while free GFP displayed diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear distribution. PMC12319013.p057.s015: These results confirm BrWRKY65’s nuclear localization, consistent with our investigation.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression enhances photosynthetic rate, transpiration, stomatal conductance and maintains stomatal aperture under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage. PMC12319013.p060.s005: Under optimal growth conditions (20°C), no significant differences were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants, confirming that StWRKY65-mediated thermotolerance is stress-inducible. PMC12319013.p060.s006: A previous report unveiled that the high-temperature stress primarily targets Photosystem II (PSII) and Rubisco, with additional effects on Cytochrome b559 (Cytb559) and plastoquinone (PQ). PMC12319013.p060.s007: Key responses include ROS accumulation, heat shock protein production, and altered secondary metabolite synthesis.

events.event_label: High temperature stress damages PSII, Rubisco, Cytb559 and plastoquinone

PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage. PMC12319013.p060.s005: Under optimal growth conditions (20°C), no significant differences were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants, confirming that StWRKY65-mediated thermotolerance is stress-inducible. PMC12319013.p060.s006: A previous report unveiled that the high-temperature stress primarily targets Photosystem II (PSII) and Rubisco, with additional effects on Cytochrome b559 (Cytb559) and plastoquinone (PQ). PMC12319013.p060.s007: Key responses include ROS accumulation, heat shock protein production, and altered secondary metabolite synthesis.

events.event_label: high temperatures damage chloroplast structures and reduce light-harvesting efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s006: A previous report unveiled that the high-temperature stress primarily targets Photosystem II (PSII) and Rubisco, with additional effects on Cytochrome b559 (Cytb559) and plastoquinone (PQ). PMC12319013.p060.s007: Key responses include ROS accumulation, heat shock protein production, and altered secondary metabolite synthesis. PMC12319013.p060.s008: Additionally, high temperatures damage the chloroplast Structure, such as thylakoid membrane destabilization, which leads to unstacking of grana and reduced light-harvesting efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s009: Furthermore, stroma lamellae dilation disrupts electron transport chain organization, and lipid peroxidation degrades membrane integrity due to ROS accumulation. PMC12319013.p060.s010: These findings align with previous research demonstrating that AtWRKY30 overexpression in wheat enhances gas exchange parameters (Pn, Gs, E) and relative water content (RWC) under combined heat and drought stress.

events.event_label: Heat stress impairs photosynthetic apparatus, efficiency and stomatal function
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s001: Extreme heat stress disrupts the photosynthetic apparatus in plant cells, leading to a significant decline in photosynthetic efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s002: Our findings reveal that sustained exposure to elevated temperatures (30–35°C) induced significant photosynthetic impairment in both RNAi and NT potato plants. PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases antioxidant enzyme activities (APX, POD, CAT, SOD)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). PMC12319013.p059.s007: In contrast, StWRKY65 RNAi lines showed the opposite trend.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression enhances growth traits and heat tolerance; RNAi reduces plant height
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: IlWRKY70 overexpression confers tolerance to salinity and drought, improving germination, root elongation and fresh weight
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s009: Similarly, under NaCl (50–100 mM) stress application, MfWRKY40-OE lines A and C showed significantly longer primary roots (1.21–1.50-fold of WT). PMC12319013.p058.s010: Similarly, another study has investigated the heterologous overexpression of IlWRKY70 in Nicotiana tabacum, which confers enhanced tolerance to NaCl-induced salinity and drought stress, as demonstrated by significantly improved germination rates, increased root elongation, and biomass accumulation (fresh weight) relative to the WT controls. PMC12319013.p058.s011: Importantly, the increase in fresh weight aligns with our findings.

events.event_label: WRKY TFs are widely distributed in plants
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s001: WRKY TFs are widely distributed in plants, such as Arabidopsis, tobacco, rice, tomato, wheat, and maize, and play key regulatory roles in various life processes and stress responses as part of the plant stress signaling pathway. PMC12319013.p056.s002: Despite the critical role of WRKY TFs in abiotic stress responses, their functional characterization in potato, the world’s fourth most important staple crop, remains poorly understood, particularly under heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p056.s003: Here, we investigated the function of StWRKY65 under different heat stresses through transgenic techniques to elucidate its regulatory role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p056.s004: In this study, we initially investigated the sequence alignment analysis, which revealed that StWRKY65 contains a canonical WRKYGQK domain at its N-terminus, along with a C2H2-type zinc-finger motif (CX5-C-X23-H-X1-H), consistent with its classification as a Group II WRKY transcription factor ( Figure 1A ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 downregulates multiple stress‑responsive genes under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

events.event_label: Heat stress upregulates antioxidant genes in StWRKY65‑OE lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s001: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in response to varying temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), we analyzed the expression of stress-responsive genes in two potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’), including StAPX, StCAT1,​StCAT2, tPOD12, StPOD47 StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3,​StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p050.s002: As shown in Figures 8A–T , under 20°C, no significant differences in the relative mRNA expression levels of these antioxidant-related genes were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants in either cultivar. PMC12319013.p050.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, the StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited​significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes, including StAPX ( Figures 8A, B ), StCAT1 ( Figures 8C, D ) StCAT2 ( Figures 8E, F ), StPOD12 ( Figures 8G, H ), StPOD47 ( Figures 8I, J ), StFeSOD2 ( Figures 8K, L ), StFeSOD3 ( Figures 8M, N ), StMnSOD ( Figures 8O, P ), StCuZnSOD1 ( Figures 8Q, R ), and StCuZnSOD2 ( Figures 8S, T ), compared to NT controls. PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases and RNAi decreases plant growth traits under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ). PMC12319013.p042.s005: Under severe 35°C heat stress, the thermoprotective effect of StWRKY65 in ‘Atlantic’ became more pronounced: OE lines displayed remarkable stress resistance, with plant height increased by 51.10%–64.90%, plant fresh and dry weights by 49.38%–57.67% and 36.11%–36.93%, respectively, root fresh and dry weights by 46.42%–63.97% and 43.15%–57.38%, respectively, and tuber weight by 77.56%–97.53% relative to NT controls. PMC12319013.p042.s006: Conversely, RNAi lines were highly sensitive to heat stress, showing drastic reductions in plant height (51.38%–61.70%), plant fresh and dry weights (57.77%–65.16% and 50.61%–59.24%), root fresh and dry weights (46.53%–56.27% and 43.28%–57.82%), and tuber weight (92.32%–94.91%) ( Figures 4A–F ), compared to NT lines. PMC12319013.p042.s007: Similar trends were observed in ‘Desiree’ plants under...

events.event_label: StWRKY65 mitigates oxidative stress by regulating antioxidant enzyme genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s006: Conversely, compared to NT controls, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) exhibited significant downregulation of these antioxidant-related genes. PMC12319013.p050.s007: These findings demonstrate that StWRKY65 plays a critical role in regulating multiple antioxidant enzyme genes under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), thereby mitigating oxidative stress responses induced by elevated temperatures.

PMC12319013.p057.s002: For example, in pepper (Capsicum annuum), CaWRKY40 is upregulated under heat stress and acts as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. events.event_label: StWRKY65 expression in leaves is induced by PEG and correlates with drought stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p032.s001: This study systematically analyzed the expression profiles of the StWRKY65 gene in leaves of two potato cultivars (‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’) under various abiotic stress conditions ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p032.s002: Under drought stress, StWRKY65 exhibited expression patterns correlated with stress severity and cultivar specificity. PMC12319013.p032.s003: In ‘Desiree’, 10% PEG6000 treatment induced a stable upregulation (with relatively steady increases at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 48 h post-treatment, peaking at 6 h), whereas 20% PEG6000 triggered fluctuating upregulation (rapid induction at 1 h, followed by a decline at 3 h, then gradual increases at 6, 12, and 24 h, reaching a peak at 24 h) ( Figures 2A–C ), suggesting its positive regulatory role under severe drought. PMC12319013.p032.s004: In ‘Atlantic’, 10% PEG6000 elicited oscillatory expression (upregulation at 1, 3, 24, and 48 h but downregulation at 6 and 12 h), while 20% PEG6000 induced variable upregulation (peaking at 12 h) ( Figures 2B–D ), indicating its potential involvement in drought resistance.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 mitigates heat stress‑induced decline in photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s005: The contrasting responses between overexpression and knockout genotypes strongly support the regulatory role of StWRKY65 in maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under high-temperature stress (30°C and 35°C).

events.event_label: PtWRKY2 is leaf‑expressed, induced by heat stress and contributes to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s006: These findings establish StWRKY65 as a potential mediator of heat stress adaptation in potato. PMC12319013.p057.s007: Our study showed a similar research trend to previous research on Pinellia ternata that revealed tissue-specific expression of five PtWRKY genes, with PtWRKY1/3/4/5 exhibiting the highest expression in roots and PtWRKY2 in leaves. PMC12319013.p057.s008: Under heat stress, only PtWRKY2 was strongly induced (200-fold at 12 h), while others were suppressed, suggesting its key role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: Heat stress alters photosynthetic traits in OE and RNAi lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s001: Analysis of gas exchange parameters revealed significant variations in both ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ potato cultivars under different magnitudes of heat stress applications (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C). PMC12319013.p053.s002: As shown in Figures 9A–F , under control conditions (20°C), there were no significant changes (P>0.05) in net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, or transpiration rate between transgenic (OE lines and RNAi lines) and NT plants of both cultivars. PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters.

events.event_label: ZmWRKY65 shows evolutionary association with AtWRKY3, OsWRKY96 and SiWRKY3
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s006: Our investigation is supported by a previous report, which explored that ZmWRKY65, a candidate WRKY TF identified from drought and SA-treated maize transcriptomic data, encodes a 516-amino acid protein featuring a conserved WRKYGQK domain, an N-terminal coiled-coil region (aa 102–142), and a C2H2 zinc-finger motif (CX5CX23 HX1H). PMC12319013.p056.s007: Phylogenetic analysis (MEGA 5.1, neighbor-joining) of 15 homologs from diverse species and 14 maize WRKYs (Phytozome 12.1) revealed close evolutionary relationships between ZmWRKY65 and AtWRKY3 (Arabidopsis), OsWRKY96 (rice), SiWRKY3 (millet), and GmWRKY33 (soybean), suggesting functional conservation among them.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression elevates photosynthetic and gas exchange traits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage. PMC12319013.p060.s005: Under optimal growth conditions (20°C), no significant differences were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants, confirming that StWRKY65-mediated thermotolerance is stress-inducible. PMC12319013.p060.s006: A previous report unveiled that the high-temperature stress primarily targets Photosystem II (PSII) and Rubisco, with additional effects on Cytochrome b559 (Cytb559) and plastoquinone (PQ). PMC12319013.p060.s007: Key responses include ROS accumulation, heat shock protein production, and altered secondary metabolite synthesis.

events.event_label: heat stress alters gas exchange parameters
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s001: Analysis of gas exchange parameters revealed significant variations in both ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ potato cultivars under different magnitudes of heat stress applications (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C). PMC12319013.p053.s002: As shown in Figures 9A–F , under control conditions (20°C), there were no significant changes (P>0.05) in net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, or transpiration rate between transgenic (OE lines and RNAi lines) and NT plants of both cultivars. PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s005: The contrasting responses between overexpression and knockout genotypes strongly support the regulatory role of StWRKY65 in maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under high-temperature stress (30°C and 35°C).

events.event_label: Heat stress suppresses PtWRKY1 expression in roots
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s006: These findings establish StWRKY65 as a potential mediator of heat stress adaptation in potato. PMC12319013.p057.s007: Our study showed a similar research trend to previous research on Pinellia ternata that revealed tissue-specific expression of five PtWRKY genes, with PtWRKY1/3/4/5 exhibiting the highest expression in roots and PtWRKY2 in leaves. PMC12319013.p057.s008: Under heat stress, only PtWRKY2 was strongly induced (200-fold at 12 h), while others were suppressed, suggesting its key role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: MfWRKY40 promotes longer root growth under mannitol and NaCl stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines. PMC12319013.p058.s008: However, MfWRKY40-OE lines A and C maintained longer roots (1.19–1.75-fold of WT). PMC12319013.p058.s009: Similarly, under NaCl (50–100 mM) stress application, MfWRKY40-OE lines A and C showed significantly longer primary roots (1.21–1.50-fold of WT). PMC12319013.p058.s010: Similarly, another study has investigated the heterologous overexpression of IlWRKY70 in Nicotiana tabacum, which confers enhanced tolerance to NaCl-induced salinity and drought stress, as demonstrated by significantly improved germination rates, increased root elongation, and biomass accumulation (fresh weight) relative to the WT controls.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 participates in heat stress responses
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p040.s001: To elucidate the functional role of StWRKY65 in heat stress responses, we generated stable transgenic potato lines with either StWRKY65-overexpression or RNAi-mediated knockdown in two cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’). PMC12319013.p040.s002: The success of genetic transformation was confirmed by qRT-PCR analysis: OE lines exhibited 11- to 19.9-fold upregulation (***P < 0.001) of StWRKY65 transcripts, while RNAi lines showed 54-77% suppression (***P < 0.001; Supplementary Figure S1 ). PMC12319013.p040.s003: For subsequent thermotolerance assays, we strategically selected transgenic lines from each cultivar based on expression profiles: (1) three high-expression lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) ( Supplementary Figures S1A, B ) displaying maximal relative transcript accumulation of StWRKY65; and (2) three effective knockdown lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) ( Supplementary Figures S1C, D ) demonstrating the strongest transcriptional suppression. PMC12319013.p040.s004: The rigorous selection of lines with significantly distinct genetic backgrounds provides a robust guarantee for the precise dissection of the regulatory functions of StWRKY65 in heat stress responses.

events.event_label: CaWRKY40 regulates thermotolerance

PMC12319013.p005.s017: In pepper (Capsicum annuum), CaWRKY40 enhances thermotolerance, with overexpression in tobacco improving heat resistance while its loss reduces tolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s018: However, the specific functions and regulatory mechanisms of potato WRKY family members (especially StWRKY65) in heat stress response have not yet been elucidated.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 increases activities of APX, POD and SOD
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases activities of APX, POD and SOD under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p046.s001: To investigate the physiological responses of transgenic and NT potato lines of the cultivars ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ under different temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), the key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), oxidative stress marker (H2O2 and MDA), osmoprotectant proline, and total chlorophyll content were compared between NT and transgenic lines (OE and RNAi), as shown in Figures 6 , 7 . PMC12319013.p046.s002: Under the control condition (20°C), no significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed in all the aforementioned physiological indicators between transgenic and NT plants. PMC12319013.p046.s003: Under the conditions of 30°C and 35°C, the activities of APX ( Figures 6 , 7A ), CAT ( Figures 6 , 7B ), POD ( Figures 6 , 7C ), and SOD ( Figures 6 , 7D ) in the OE lines were significantly higher than those in the NT and RNAi lines (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), indicating that the overexpression of StWRKY65 could enhance the antioxidant defense system and more efficiently scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) under heat stress. PMC12319013.p046.s004: Meanwhile, the contents of oxidative stress markers H2O2 ( Figures 6 , 7E ) and MDA ( Figures 6 , 7F ) were also analyzed.

PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s005: Under optimal growth conditions (20°C), no significant differences were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants, confirming that StWRKY65-mediated thermotolerance is stress-inducible. events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression enhances antioxidant enzymes and reduces oxidative damage under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). events.event_label: StWRKY65 regulates StCAT1/2 expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects. PMC12319013.p002.s011: This study demonstrates the pivotal role of StWRKY65 in enhancing potato thermotolerance by simultaneously boosting antioxidant defenses and maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under heat stress. PMC12319013.p002.s012: As climate change intensifies thermal extremes, these findings position StWRKY65 as a valuable genetic target for developing heat-resistant potato varieties, offering a timely strategy to protect this vital crop and global food security.

events.event_label: ZmWRKY106 confers tolerance to drought and heat
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p005.s012: In tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum), NtWRKY65 regulates the expression of genes related to salt stress. events.event_label: Heat stress impairs photosynthetic and light‑harvesting efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s001: Extreme heat stress disrupts the photosynthetic apparatus in plant cells, leading to a significant decline in photosynthetic efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s002: Our findings reveal that sustained exposure to elevated temperatures (30–35°C) induced significant photosynthetic impairment in both RNAi and NT potato plants. PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage. PMC12319013.p060.s006: A previous report unveiled that the high-temperature stress primarily targets Photosystem II (PSII) and Rubisco, with additional effects on Cytochrome b559 (Cytb559) and plastoquinone (PQ). PMC12319013.p060.s007: Key responses include ROS accumulation, heat shock protein production, and altered secondary metabolite synthesis. PMC12319013.p060.s008: Additionally, high temperatures damage the chloroplast Structure, such as thylakoid membrane destabilization, which leads to unstacking of grana and reduced light-harvesting efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s009: Furthermore, stroma lamellae dilation disrupts electron transport chain organization, and lipid peroxidation degrades membrane integrity due to ROS accumulation. PMC12319013.p060.s010: These findings align with previous research demonstrating that AtWRKY30 overexpression in wheat enhances gas exchange parameters (Pn, Gs, E) and relative water content (RWC) under combined heat and drought stress.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression enhances thermotolerance and growth traits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s001: Abiotic stress, such as extreme temperatures, disrupts plants’ key physiological and biochemical processes, ultimately hindering growth and biomass production. PMC12319013.p058.s002: To counteract these challenges, plants activate sophisticated defense mechanisms or key genes, which modulate growth patterns and restore metabolic pathways to maintain cellular homeostasis and ensure survival. PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 activates antioxidant genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects. PMC12319013.p002.s011: This study demonstrates the pivotal role of StWRKY65 in enhancing potato thermotolerance by simultaneously boosting antioxidant defenses and maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under heat stress. PMC12319013.p002.s012: As climate change intensifies thermal extremes, these findings position StWRKY65 as a valuable genetic target for developing heat-resistant potato varieties, offering a timely strategy to protect this vital crop and global food security.

events.event_label: WRKY nuclear localization
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s006: These findings establish StWRKY65 as a potential mediator of heat stress adaptation in potato. PMC12319013.p057.s007: Our study showed a similar research trend to previous research on Pinellia ternata that revealed tissue-specific expression of five PtWRKY genes, with PtWRKY1/3/4/5 exhibiting the highest expression in roots and PtWRKY2 in leaves. PMC12319013.p057.s008: Under heat stress, only PtWRKY2 was strongly induced (200-fold at 12 h), while others were suppressed, suggesting its key role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells. PMC12319013.p057.s014: Confocal microscopy showed nuclear-specific GFP fluorescence, co-localizing with DAPI-stained nuclei, while free GFP displayed diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear distribution. PMC12319013.p057.s015: These results confirm BrWRKY65’s nuclear localization, consistent with our investigation.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression upregulates stress‑responsive genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

PMC12319013.p042.s008: At 35°C, OE lines further demonstrated enhanced heat tolerance (plant height +39.12%–49.66%, plant fresh/dry weights +40.37%–48.89%/+38.06%–53.09%, root fresh/dry weights +40.32%–50.97%/+48.52%–65.38%, tuber weight +79.54%–98.74%), whereas RNAi lines suffered severe growth inhibition (plant height −38.69%–46.15%, plant fresh/dry weights −38.86%–47.60%/−39.55%–41.60%, root fresh/dry weights −41.29%–50.94%/−49.17%–61.71%, tuber weight −78.90%–95.60%), compared to NT potato lines ( Figures 5A–F ). events.event_label: Heat stress increases stomatal conductance and transpiration rate in StWRKY65-overexpressing lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s001: Analysis of gas exchange parameters revealed significant variations in both ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ potato cultivars under different magnitudes of heat stress applications (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C). PMC12319013.p053.s002: As shown in Figures 9A–F , under control conditions (20°C), there were no significant changes (P>0.05) in net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, or transpiration rate between transgenic (OE lines and RNAi lines) and NT plants of both cultivars. PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters.

events.event_label: Heat stress upregulates StAPX, StCAT1 and StPOD47 in StWRKY65-overexpressing lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s001: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in response to varying temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), we analyzed the expression of stress-responsive genes in two potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’), including StAPX, StCAT1,​StCAT2, tPOD12, StPOD47 StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3,​StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p050.s002: As shown in Figures 8A–T , under 20°C, no significant differences in the relative mRNA expression levels of these antioxidant-related genes were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants in either cultivar. PMC12319013.p050.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, the StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited​significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes, including StAPX ( Figures 8A, B ), StCAT1 ( Figures 8C, D ) StCAT2 ( Figures 8E, F ), StPOD12 ( Figures 8G, H ), StPOD47 ( Figures 8I, J ), StFeSOD2 ( Figures 8K, L ), StFeSOD3 ( Figures 8M, N ), StMnSOD ( Figures 8O, P ), StCuZnSOD1 ( Figures 8Q, R ), and StCuZnSOD2 ( Figures 8S, T ), compared to NT controls. PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions.

events.event_label: Heat stress increases dry weight and root fresh weight in StWRKY65-overexpressing lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s001: Growth index analysis demonstrated significant differences in heat tolerance between transgenic and NT potato lines under distinct heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p042.s002: Under control conditions (20°C), no significant differences (P>0.05) were detected in growth indices, including plant height, plant fresh and dry weights, root fresh and dry weights, and tuber weight, among genotypes of both the ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ varieties, as illustrated in Figures 4 , 5 . PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ).

events.event_label: WRKY TFs confer tolerance to drought and salinity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p004.s012: Plants employ adaptive mechanisms, including stress-responsive TFs like MYB, AP2/ERFBP, NAC, and WRKY, to mitigate high-temperature damage. PMC12319013.p004.s013: Among these, WRKY TFs play a pivotal role in regulating pathways that combat diverse abiotic stresses, such as heat, drought, and salinity.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 is drought‑responsive in leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p032.s001: This study systematically analyzed the expression profiles of the StWRKY65 gene in leaves of two potato cultivars (‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’) under various abiotic stress conditions ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p032.s002: Under drought stress, StWRKY65 exhibited expression patterns correlated with stress severity and cultivar specificity. PMC12319013.p032.s003: In ‘Desiree’, 10% PEG6000 treatment induced a stable upregulation (with relatively steady increases at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 48 h post-treatment, peaking at 6 h), whereas 20% PEG6000 triggered fluctuating upregulation (rapid induction at 1 h, followed by a decline at 3 h, then gradual increases at 6, 12, and 24 h, reaching a peak at 24 h) ( Figures 2A–C ), suggesting its positive regulatory role under severe drought. PMC12319013.p032.s004: In ‘Atlantic’, 10% PEG6000 elicited oscillatory expression (upregulation at 1, 3, 24, and 48 h but downregulation at 6 and 12 h), while 20% PEG6000 induced variable upregulation (peaking at 12 h) ( Figures 2B–D ), indicating its potential involvement in drought resistance.

events.event_label: Heat stress affects growth
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s001: Heat stress severely impacts the growth and development of potato plants. PMC12319013.p002.s002: However, the molecular mechanisms underlying thermotolerance, particularly the role of WRKY transcription factors (TFs), remain poorly understood. PMC12319013.p002.s003: Here, we identified StWRKY65 as a heat-responsive gene in potato, demonstrating significant transcriptional upregulation under 30°C and 35°C heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p002.s004: Phylogenetic analysis classified StWRKY65 into the WRKY Group II family, revealing high evolutionary conservation with its tomato ortholog SlWRKY65.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 localizes_to nuclear targeting
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s003: Here, we identified StWRKY65 as a heat-responsive gene in potato, demonstrating significant transcriptional upregulation under 30°C and 35°C heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p002.s004: Phylogenetic analysis classified StWRKY65 into the WRKY Group II family, revealing high evolutionary conservation with its tomato ortholog SlWRKY65. PMC12319013.p002.s005: Subcellular localization confirmed its nuclear targeting. PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 decreases_expression_of StPOD12/47
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects. PMC12319013.p002.s011: This study demonstrates the pivotal role of StWRKY65 in enhancing potato thermotolerance by simultaneously boosting antioxidant defenses and maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under heat stress. PMC12319013.p002.s012: As climate change intensifies thermal extremes, these findings position StWRKY65 as a valuable genetic target for developing heat-resistant potato varieties, offering a timely strategy to protect this vital crop and global food security.

PMC12319013.p004.s011: Multiple studies confirm that heat stress disproportionately affects potato productivity compared to other climatic factors. PMC12319013.p004.s012: Plants employ adaptive mechanisms, including stress-responsive TFs like MYB, AP2/ERFBP, NAC, and WRKY, to mitigate high-temperature damage. events.event_label: TaWRKY33 enhances_or_improves heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p005.s012: In tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum), NtWRKY65 regulates the expression of genes related to salt stress. PMC12319013.p005.s013: In wheat (Triticum aestivum), TaWRKY33 overexpression improves heat tolerance, demonstrating WRKY TFs’ diverse roles in stress responses. PMC12319013.p005.s014: In maize (Zea mays), the overexpression of ZmWRKY40 regulates drought tolerance in transgenic Arabidopsis, and ZmWRKY106 confers drought and heat tolerance in transgenic maize. PMC12319013.p005.s015: In rice (Oryza sativa), OsWRKY11 enhanced drought tolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s016: In tomato (Solanum lycopersicum), SlWRKY80 plays a crucial role in enabling transgenic tomato plants to tolerate saline-alkali stress more effectively than wild-type plants.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 associates_with heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p006.s003: Heat stress is becoming increasingly detrimental to potato yields under climate change, and preliminary evidence suggests StWRKY65 may have unique functional properties distinct from other stress-responsive WRKYs. PMC12319013.p006.s004: Our study is the first to systematically investigate StWRKY65’s involvement in heat stress adaptation, which could reveal novel mechanisms of thermotolerance in this globally important crop. PMC12319013.p006.s005: Understanding StWRKY65’s function may provide crucial molecular targets for developing heat-resistant potato varieties through either conventional breeding or biotechnological approaches. PMC12319013.p006.s006: Focusing on the StWRKY65 gene, this study employs a multi-faceted approach, integrating sequence alignment and phylogenetic analysis, expression dynamics, subcellular localization, and transgenic assays to unravel its contribution to thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p006.s007: Our results reveal that StWRKY65 coordinates heat adaptation by regulating growth traits (plant height, tuber yield, fresh and dry weights of plants and their roots), antioxidant defenses systems (APX, SOD, POD, CAT), photosynthetic performance (Net photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance), and the induction of antioxidant enzymes related genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 is_expressed_in epidermal cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 is_expressed_in Nicotiana benthamiana
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65-GFP Fusion Protein participates_in Subcellular Localization
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p038.s001: Subcellular Localization of StWRKY65-GFP Fusion Protein. PMC12319013.p038.s002: Confocal laser scanning microscopy was conducted to analyze tobacco plants transformed with the pCAM35-GFP-StWRKY65 construct. PMC12319013.p038.s003: The empty vector expressing only GFP was used as a control. PMC12319013.p038.s004: Scale bar = 50 μm.

events.event_label: heat stress affects heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s001: Growth index analysis demonstrated significant differences in heat tolerance between transgenic and NT potato lines under distinct heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p042.s002: Under control conditions (20°C), no significant differences (P>0.05) were detected in growth indices, including plant height, plant fresh and dry weights, root fresh and dry weights, and tuber weight, among genotypes of both the ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ varieties, as illustrated in Figures 4 , 5 . PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ).
